title: container create failed: container_linux.go:336: starting container process caused "process_linux.go:293: applying cgroup configuration for process caused open /sys/fs/cgroup/cpuset/machine.slice/cpuset.cpus: no such file or directory"
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST?**:

[//]: # Uncomment only one, leave it on its own line:

# **kind bug**

[//]: # **kind feature**

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

From time to time, we are hit by a failure while starting a container:
```
2018-11-21 01:23:16 INFO | "2018-11-21 01:20:36,157 ERROR: 5978 -- container create failed: container_linux.go:336: starting container process caused \"process_linux.go:293: applying cgroup configuration for process caused \\\"open /sys/fs/cgroup/cpuset/machine.slice/cpuset.cpus: no such file or directory\\\"\""
2018-11-21 01:23:16 INFO | ": internal libpod error", 2018-11-21 01:23:16 INFO | "",
```

We don't have much more logs unfortunately. The action is "podman run", just after we have pulled the image.
The failure hit randomly any container, so it's not an issue related to the image nor way we run it.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. It's a random failure, we didn't find any trigger for that :(.

2.

3.

**Describe the results you received:**
Sometimes `podman run' fails.

**Describe the results you expected:**
It should have a consistent behaviour and run the container without random failure

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:       0.11.1.1
Go Version:    go1.10.2
OS/Arch:       linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info`:**


**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
So the "run" command is pretty long, but doesn't have much subtle things:
we have a couple of --env var set, a nice list of bind-mounts (over 10). We run the container as ```--user root```, and set a ```--entrypoint``` script.

In some cases, we also add ```--privileged```, but as the command is generated[1], it's not that easy to get the one for each of the failure we have - especially since they are hitting at random.


[1] https://github.com/openstack/tripleo-heat-templates/blob/master/docker/docker-puppet.py#L364-L416

This is happening in down in runc, and obviously runc is not finding the file under cgroups.

@mheon 
open /sys/fs/cgroup/cpuset/machine.slice/cpuset.cpus

Is this the usual location that we tell runc to use?
@mheon I can confirm we don't push anything in libpod.conf. It's the vanilla file from the RPM.

Great, thanks.

So this definitely isn't a path we passed into runc, @rhatdan - it's looking at cgroupfs itself

https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/blob/76520a4bf07b0558aca3b867277d696b41978b44/libcontainer/cgroups/systemd/apply_systemd.go#L215

This is what's erroring

I think it must be doing some raw parsing of /sys/fs/cgroup to apply the right cgroup, despite it being the systemd driver

@cjeanner Possible you can get some syslog messages out of journalctl on a VM that failed? Would like to see if there's anything similar to the following:

`Nov 21 11:05:10 courageous.lldp.net systemd[1]: libcontainer-8068-systemd-test-default-dependencies.scope: Scope has no PIDs. Refusing.
`
`
Nov 21 11:05:10 courageous.lldp.net systemd[1]: libcontainer-8068-systemd-test-default-dependencies.scope: Scope has no PIDs. Refusing.
`

When containers are starting... would verify that this is indeed the systemd driver failing (runc does some probes to verify it can make cgroups before starting a container, these errors prove that the probes are happening and the code path is what I think it is)

I can see:
```Nov 21 01:20:35 undercloud.localdomain systemd[1]: Scope libcontainer-8929-systemd-test-default-dependencies.scope has no PIDs. Refusing.```
Alright, so this is definitely the systemd driver then - good to know I'm looking in the right place

Theory: #1844 and #1817 are somehow related - three errors accessing files on the same CI VMs.

Any chance you can preserve a VM after it fails and get us access to it? I'm curious to see what the state of a container's rootfs is once a failure happens.

@EmilienM @cjeanner I tried yesterday afternoon, but I can't seem to reproduce locally.

I'm still somewhat convinced that these issues are all somehow related, but they also don't seem to share any code paths on our end, which makes me think it's an environment issue of some sort.

@cjeanner gave me configs for reproducing the Openstack CI VMs on which these are seen. I've run the suite a few times, but haven't seen these yet.

@EmilienM @cjeanner how often are you folks seeing this? Once every 2-3 runs? Once every 10 runs?
